content:
You are the Accumulator. Given one paper (markdown + optionally a cloned
GitHub repo), do TWO things:
  1. Assign the paper to ONE of the 7 ML domains below.
  2. Extract 1–3 distinct main contributions as seeds.

Each seed captures (a) the underlying problem the paper addresses, and
(b) the method that solves it, including what is special / novel about
the approach. Avoid splitting one idea into multiple sub-seeds; prefer
fewer, higher-quality seeds.

## Domains (pick exactly one)
- LLM_NLP    : language models, NLP, text-only agents
- MULTIMODAL : cross-modality (text+image, text+audio, etc.).
               Vision-Language Models (VLM, e.g., CLIP / LLaVA / GPT-4V)
               belong HERE, not under CV.
- CV         : visual perception (classification, detection, segmentation,
               video understanding) AND image/video generation
               (diffusion, GAN, VAE) when the contribution is the
               generative mechanism on visual data.
- RL         : reinforcement learning algorithms, policies, environments;
               RLHF when the contribution is the RL algorithm itself.
- IR_REC     : information retrieval, recommender systems, search.
- SPEECH     : speech / audio (TTS, ASR, audio language models).
- ROBOTICS   : embodied agents, manipulation, planning in physical world.
               If a paper proposes an embodied policy, prefer ROBOTICS
               over RL.

## Workspace contract
Paper directory:    /work/2204.10872
paper.md            Pre-converted markdown of the PDF.
repo/               Will exist if you `git clone` it. Otherwise unavailable.

## Tools
- Read       : view paper.md and any file under the paper directory
- Grep       : search inside paper.md or the cloned repo
- Bash       : `git clone <url> /work/2204.10872/repo` (only if you've found a
               *clearly official* GitHub URL in the paper);
               also general filesystem exploration: tree / ls / find / wc

## Output (terminate the run)
When you have finished, stop calling tools and reply with a SINGLE JSON
object inside a ```json fenced block. No extra prose:
  ```json
  {"title": "...", "domain": "<one of 7>", "seeds": [{"problem": "...", "method": "..."}, ...]}
  ```
- title  = the paper's actual title (NOT a venue label or page header)
- domain = exactly one of LLM_NLP / MULTIMODAL / CV / RL / IR_REC / SPEECH / ROBOTICS
- seeds  = 1–3 entries, or empty list if extraction failed

## Default flow
1. Read paper.md end-to-end. Identify the paper's true title (often the
   first H1, but PDF→md sometimes places venue labels / preprint headers
   above it — pick the actual title, not the noise). For longer papers
   a single Read may not cover everything; continue with Read(offset=...)
   until you actually understand the methodology and experiments
   sections, not just the abstract and introduction.
2. Decide the domain from the 7 above. Use the tie-breakers in the list
   when a paper sits between two.
3. Look for an official GitHub URL (abstract, footnote 1, "Code:" line, etc.).
   If you find one and it is unambiguously the paper's repo, clone it.
   If you are unsure, do NOT clone — proceed paper-only.
4. (If repo cloned) Use Bash / Grep to locate where the main contribution
   is implemented. Read enough to confirm what's special.
5. Draft 1–3 candidate seeds in your reasoning. Each seed:
     - problem: 1–2 sentences. The challenge the paper addresses.
     - method: 3–5 sentences. The solution AND what is special about it.
6. BEFORE emitting the final JSON, verify each candidate seed against
   the paper. For each seed:
     - Identify 2-3 specific claims your `method` text makes
       (mechanism name, key design choice, quantitative finding).
     - Use Grep on paper.md to locate where each claim is supported,
       and Read(offset=...) to inspect the surrounding context if the
       Grep snippet is insufficient. (Use repo Grep/Read if cloned.)
     - Revise the seed text to match the paper. If a claim cannot be
       grounded after re-reading, REMOVE that claim or drop the seed
       entirely.
   This step is MANDATORY: do not skip it because a seed feels right.
7. Stop calling tools and emit the final JSON object described under
   "## Output". This terminates the run.

Budget: 100 tool calls. Plan first, then act.

## Paper
Paper directory: /work/2204.10872

Begin by reading paper.md.

1	## Learning-to-Rank at the Speed of Sampling: Plackett-Luce Gradient Estimation With Minimal Computational Complexity
     2	
     3	Harrie Oosterhuis Radboud University Nijmegen, The Netherlands
     4	
     5	harrie.oosterhuis@ru.nl
     6	
     7	category does not have issues with differentiability or smoothness because their methods optimize a probabilistic distribution over rankings. Recently, probabilistic ranking models have also received additional attention for their applicability to ranking fairness tasks [8]. Since probabilistic models are able to divide exposure over items more fairly than deterministic models.
     8	
     9	## ABSTRACT
    10	
    11	Plackett-Luce gradient estimation enables the optimization of stochastic ranking models within feasible time constraints through sampling techniques. Unfortunately, the computational complexity of existing methods does not scale well with the length of the rankings, i.e. the ranking cutoff, nor with the item collection size.
    12	
    13	In this paper, we introduce the novel PL-Rank-3 algorithm that performs unbiased gradient estimation with a computational complexity comparable to the best sorting algorithms. As a result, our novel learning-to-rank method is applicable in any scenario where standard sorting is feasible in reasonable time. Our experimental results indicate large gains in the time required for optimization, without any loss in performance. For the field, our contribution could potentially allow state-of-the-art learning-to-rank methods to be applied to much larger scales than previously feasible.
    14	
    15	## CCS CONCEPTS
    16	
    17	· Information systems → Learning to rank .
    18	
    19	## KEYWORDS
    20	
    21	Learning to Rank; Policy Gradients; Computational Complexity
    22	
    23	## ACMReference Format:
    24	
    25	Harrie Oosterhuis. 2022. Learning-to-Rank at the Speed of Sampling: PlackettLuce Gradient Estimation With Minimal Computational Complexity. In Proceedings of the 45th Int'l ACM SIGIR Conference on Research and Development in Information Retrieval (SIGIR '22), July 11-15, 2022, Madrid, Spain. ACM, New York, NY, USA, 6 pages. https://doi.org/10.1145/3477495.3531842
    26	
    27	## 1 INTRODUCTION
    28	
    29	Learning-to-Rank (LTR) methods optimize ranking systems for search and recommendation purposes [15]. In the field of Information Retrieval (IR), they are generally deployed to maximize well-known ranking metrics such as: Discounted Cumulative Gain (DCG) or precision [13]. The main difficulty with the LTR task is that ranking metrics are non-differentiable, discrete and very non-smooth due to the underlying sorting process [4]. In broad terms, the existing solutions to this problem can be divided into two categories: heuristic bounds or approximations of ranking metrics or their gradients [3, 4, 10, 14, 22, 24]; and optimizing a probabilistic ranking model instead of a deterministic model [5, 18, 23, 25]. The latter
    30	
    31	<!-- image -->
    32	
    33	[This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution International 4.0 License.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)
    34	
    35	SIGIR '22, July 11-15, 2022, Madrid, Spain. © 2022 Copyright held by the owner/author(s). ACM ISBN 978-1-4503-8732-3/22/07.
    36	
    37	https://doi.org/10.1145/3477495.3531842
    38	
    39	Oosterhuis [18] introduced the PL-Rank method to efficiently optimize Plackett-Luce (PL) ranking models: a specific type of ranking model based on decision theory [16, 19]. They use Gumbel sampling [2, 11] to quickly sample many rankings from a PL ranking model, and subsequently, apply the PL-Rank-1 or PL-Rank-2 algorithm to these samples to unbiasedly approximate the gradient of a ranking metric w.r.t. the model. While PL-Rank provides a significant contribution to the LTR field, we recognize two shortcomings in the work of Oosterhuis [18]: neither PL-Rank-1 or PL-Rank-2 scale well with long rankings; and they do not compare PL-Rank to the earlier and comparable StochasticRank algorithm [23].
    40	
    41	This paper addresses both of these shortcomings: our main contribution is the novel PL-Rank-3 algorithm that computes the same approximation as PL-Rank-2 while minimizing its computational complexity. PL-Rank-3 has computional costs in the same order as the best sorting algorithms; we posit that this is the lowest order possible for a metric-based LTR method. Our experimental comparison includes both the previous PL-Rank-2 and StochasticRank as baselines, providing the first comparison between these two methods. The introduction of PL-Rank-3 is exciting for the LTR field, as it pushes the limit of the minimal computational complexity that LTR methods can have. Potentially, it may enable future LTR to be applied to much larger scales than currently feasible.
    42

   140	
   141	## 4 EXPERIMENTAL SETUP
   142	
   143	We experimentally evaluate how the improvements in computational complexity translate to improvements in practical costs. Our experimental runs optimize the 𝐷𝐶𝐺 @ 𝐾 of neural ranking models on the Yahoo! Webscope-Set1 [6], MSLR-Web30k [20] and Istella [7] datasets. The neural models have two-hidden layers of 32 sigmoid activation nodes, backpropagation via standard gradient descent with a learning rate of 0 . 01 was applied using Tensorflow [1]. We compare our PL-Rank-3 algorithm, with PL-Rank2 [18] and StochasticRank [23]. StochasticRank was chosen as a baseline because it shares many properties with PL-Rank (see Table 1) yet was not compared with PL-Rank in previous work [18]. Our StochasticRank implementation uses the Gumbel distribution as stochastic noise instead of the normal distribution of the original algorithm [23], this makes the method applicable and effective to optimize a PL model. We reimplemented the PL-Rank and StochasticRank algorithms in Numpy [12] and performed all our experiments on AMD EPYC™ 7H12 CPUs. 2 The ranking length/metric cutoff was varied: 𝐾 ∈ { 5 , 10 , 25 , 50 , 100 } and number of sampled rankings: 𝑁 ∈ { 100 , 1000 } , to measure their impact on computational costs. Performance was measured over 200 minutes of training time, in addition to the average time required to complete one training epoch. All reported results are averages over 30 independent runs performed under identical circumstances. We report Normalized 𝐷𝐶𝐺 @ 𝐾 (NDCG@K) as our ranking performance metric computed on the held-out test-set of each dataset; following the advice of Ferrante et al. [9], we do not use querynormalization but dataset-normalization: we divide the 𝐷𝐶𝐺 @ 𝐾 of a ranking model by the maximum possible 𝐷𝐶𝐺 @ 𝐾 value on the entire test-set of the dataset.

All displayed results are averages over 30 independent runs. NDCG was normalized on dataset-level instead of query-level [9].
   148

   150	
   151	## 5 RESULTS
   152	
   153	Figure 1 and Table 2 shows the effect of ranking length 𝐾 on the computational costs of the LTR methods. As expected, Figure 1 reveals that PL-Rank-2 and StochasticRank are heavily affected by increases in 𝐾 : there appears to be a clear linear trend on the MSLR and Istella datasets. We note that many queries in the Yahoo! dataset have less than 100 documents ( 𝐷 ≤ 100) which could explain why the effect is sub-linear on that dataset. In contrast, PL-Rank-3 appears barely affected by 𝐾 on all of the datasets.
   154	
   155	Table 2 allows us to also compare the computational costs in more detail. Regardless of whether 𝑁 = 100 or 𝑁 = 1000, the required times of PL-Rank-2 and StochasticRank scale close to linearly with 𝐾 , but those for PL-Rank-3 do not. For instance, on Istella with 𝑁 = 100 and 𝐾 = 5, PL-Rank-2 needs 1.96 minutes, StochasticRank needs 2.79 and PL-Rank-3 needs 1.24 minutes, when compared to 𝐾 = 100, PL-Rank-2 needs an additional 25 minutes, StochasticRank 49 minutes more but PL-Rank-3 only requires an increase of 28 seconds. Moreover, PL-Rank-3 has the lowest computational costs when compared to the other methods with the same 𝑁 and 𝐾 values, across all three datasets. We thus conclude that in terms of time required to complete a single epoch, PL-Rank-3 is a clear improvement over PL-Rank-2 and StochasticRank. Additionally, in terms of scalability of computational costs w.r.t. 𝐾 , PL-Rank-3 is the best choice by a considerable margin.
   156	
   157	Nowthat we have established that PL-Rank-3 completes training epochs considerably faster than the other methods, we consider its effect on how quickly certain performance can be reached. Figure 2 show the performance of the LTR methods when optimizing 𝐷𝐶𝐺 @ 𝐾 over 200 minutes for various 𝐾 values. In all but two scenarios, PL-Rank-3 provides the highest performance at all times, where it seems to mostly depend on 𝐾 whether 𝑁 = 100 or 𝑁 = 1000 is a better choice. In particular, when 𝐾 ∈ { 5 , 10 } PL-Rank-3 with 𝑁 = 100 has the highest performance on MSLR and Istella and is only outperformed by PL-Rank-3 with 𝑁 = 1000 on Yahoo! Conversely, when 𝐾 ∈ { 50 , 100 } , 𝑁 = 1000 is the better choice for PL-Rank-3 on all datasets. This suggests that gradient estimation for larger values of 𝐾 is more prone to variance and thus requires more samples for stable optimization. Overall, PL-Rank-2 appears very affected by variance across datasets and 𝐾 values, we mostly attribute this to the small number of epochs it can complete in 200 minutes. For example, on Istella with 𝐾 = 100, PL-Rank-2 completes less than eight epochs whereas PL-Rank-3 can complete 116 epochs. Interestingly, StochasticRank with 𝑁 = 100 has stable performance that is sometimes comparable with PL-Rank-3 with 𝑁 = 1000 on the Yahoo! dataset. This indicates that the sample-efficiency of StochasticRank is actually better than PL-Rank-3, however, due to its low computational costs, PL-Rank-3 still outperforms it on MSLR and Istella and when 𝐾 ∈ { 5 , 10 , 20 } on Yahoo! We conclude that PL-Rank-3 provides a clear and substantial improvement over PL-Rank-2, and in most scenarios also outperforms StochasticRank.
   158	
   159	## 6 CONCLUSION
   160	
   161	We have introduced PL-Rank-3 an LTR algorithm to estimate the gradient of a PL ranking model with the same computational complexity as the best sorting algorithms. PL-Rank-3 could enable future metric-based LTR to be applicable to ranking lengths and item collection sizes of much larger scales than previously feasible.
   162	
   163	## Code and data
   164	
   165	To facilitate reproducibility, this work only made use of publicly available data and our experimental implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/HarrieO/2022-SIGIR-plackett-luce.

13:In this paper, we introduce the novel PL-Rank-3 algorithm that performs unbiased gradient estimation with a computational complexity comparable to the best sorting algorithms. As a result, our novel learning-to-rank method is applicable in any scenario where standard sorting is feasible in reasonable time. Our experimental results indicate large gains in the time required for optimization, without any loss in performance. For the field, our contribution could potentially allow state-of-the-art learning-to-rank methods to be applied to much larger scales than previously feasible.
39:Oosterhuis [18] introduced the PL-Rank method to efficiently optimize Plackett-Luce (PL) ranking models: a specific type of ranking model based on decision theory [16, 19]. They use Gumbel sampling [2, 11] to quickly sample many rankings from a PL ranking model, and subsequently, apply the PL-Rank-1 or PL-Rank-2 algorithm to these samples to unbiasedly approximate the gradient of a ranking metric w.r.t. the model. While PL-Rank provides a significant contribution to the LTR field, we recognize two shortcomings in the work of Oosterhuis [18]: neither PL-Rank-1 or PL-Rank-2 scale well with long rankings; and they do not compare PL-Rank to the earlier and comparable StochasticRank algorithm [23].

# Learning-to-Rank at the Speed of Sampling: Plackett-Luce Gradient Estimation With Minimal Computational Complexity
This repository contains the code used for the experiments in "Learning-to-Rank at the Speed of Sampling: Plackett-Luce Gradient Estimation With Minimal Computational Complexity" published at SIGIR 2022 ([available here](https://harrieo.github.io//publication/2022-sigir-short)).

Citation
--------

If you use this code to produce results for your scientific publication, or if you share a copy or fork, please refer to our SIGIR 2022 paper:
```
@inproceedings{oosterhuis2022plrank,
  Author = {Oosterhuis, Harrie},
  Booktitle = {Proceedings of the 45th International ACM SIGIR Conference on Research and Development in Information Retrieval (SIGIR`22)},
  Organization = {ACM},
  Title = {Learning-to-Rank at the Speed of Sampling: Plackett-Luce Gradient Estimation With Minimal Computational Complexity},
  Year = {2022}
}
```

License
-------

The contents of this repository are licensed under the [MIT license](LICENSE). If you modify its contents in any way, please link back to this repository.

Usage
-------

This code makes use of [Python 3](https://www.python.org/), the [numpy](https://numpy.org/) and the [tensorflow](https://www.tensorflow.org/) packages, make sure they are installed.

A file is required that explains the location and details of the LTR datasets available on the system, for the Yahoo! Webscope, MSLR-Web30k, and Istella datasets an example file is available. Copy the file:
```
cp example_datasets_info.txt local_dataset_info.txt
```
Open this copy and edit the paths to the folders where the train/test/vali files are placed. (Note that the Istella dataset does not have a validation set by default, I recommend partitioning 10% from the training data to creat a validation set.)

Here are some command-line examples that illustrate how the results in the paper can be replicated.
First create a folder to store the resulting models:
```
mkdir local_output
```
The experiments are all based on *run.py* with the *--loss* flag to indicate the loss to use: PL_rank_2/PL_rank_3/stochasticrank_PL (the losses from the [SIGIR`21 PL-rank paper](https://harrieo.github.io//publication/2021-plrank) are also implemented); *--cutoff* indicates the top-k that is being optimized, e.g. 5 for DCG@5; *--num_samples* the number of samples to use per gradient estimation (with *dynamic* for a dynamic strategy); *--dataset* indicates the dataset name, e.g. *Webscope_C14_Set1*.
The following command optimizes DCG@5 with PL-Rank-2 and with 100 samples on the Yahoo! dataset:
```
python3 run.py local_output/yahoo_ndcg5_dynamic_plrank2.txt --num_samples 100 --loss PL_rank_3 --cutoff 5 --dataset Webscope_C14_Set1
```

     4	import numpy as np
     5	import utils.plackettluce as pl
     6	
     7	def gumbel_stochasticrank(rank_weights, labels, scores, n_samples=None, sampled_rankings=None, sampled_inv_rankings=None, sampled_values=None):
     8	  n_docs = labels.shape[0]
     9	  result = np.zeros(n_docs, dtype=np.float64)
    10	  cutoff = min(rank_weights.shape[0], n_docs-1)
    11	
    12	  if n_docs == 1:
    13	    return np.zeros_like(scores)
    14	
    15	  assert n_samples is not None or sampled_rankings is not None
    16	  if sampled_rankings is None:
    17	    (sampled_rankings,
    18	     _,_,_,
    19	     sampled_values) = pl.gumbel_sample_rankings(
    20	                                    scores,
    21	                                    n_samples,
    22	                                    cutoff=cutoff+1,
    23	                                    return_gumbel=True)
    24	  else:
    25	    n_samples = sampled_rankings.shape[0]
    26	
    27	  delta_weights = rank_weights[:cutoff].copy()
    28	  if rank_weights.shape[0] < n_docs:
    29	    delta_weights[:-1] -= rank_weights[1:cutoff]
    30	  else:
    31	    delta_weights -= rank_weights[1:cutoff+1]
    32	
    33	  ranking_values = sampled_values[np.arange(n_samples)[:,None], sampled_rankings]
    34	  ranking_scores = scores[sampled_rankings[:,:cutoff]]
    35	
    36	  pairwise_diff = ranking_values[:,:cutoff,None] - scores[None,None,:]
    37	  pairwise_pdf = np.exp(-pairwise_diff-np.exp(-pairwise_diff))
    38	
    39	  sampled_labels = labels[sampled_rankings]
    40	  delta_pairwise = delta_weights[None,:,None]*(labels[None,None,:] - sampled_labels[:,:cutoff,None])
    41	
    42	  sampled_grad = pairwise_pdf*delta_pairwise
    43	  sampled_mask = np.greater_equal(sampled_values[:,None,:], ranking_values[:,:cutoff,None])
    44	  sampled_grad[sampled_mask] = 0
    45	  sampled_grad = np.sum(sampled_grad, axis=1)
    46	
    47	  pairwise_diff = ranking_values[:,1:,None] - ranking_scores[:,None,:]
    48	  pairwise_pdf = np.exp(-pairwise_diff-np.exp(-pairwise_diff))
    49	  delta_moved = delta_weights[None,:,None]*(sampled_labels[:,None,:cutoff] - sampled_labels[:,1:,None])
    50	
    51	  k_range = np.arange(cutoff)
    52	  k_mask = np.less_equal(k_range[:,None],k_range[None,:])[None,:,:]
    53	  grad_cor = pairwise_pdf*np.where(k_mask, delta_moved, 0)
    54	  grad_cor = np.sum(grad_cor, axis=1)
    55	
    56	  np.add.at(sampled_grad, (np.arange(n_samples)[:,None], sampled_rankings[:,:cutoff]), grad_cor)
    57	
    58	  return np.mean(sampled_grad, axis=0)
    59	
    60	def normal_pdf(x):
    61	  return np.exp(-x**2./(2.))/(2.*np.pi*1.)**.5

    63	def normal_stochasticrank(rank_weights, labels, scores, n_samples=None, sampled_rankings=None, sampled_inv_rankings=None, sampled_values=None):

    74	  if sampled_rankings is None:

    76	     sampled_values) = pl.normal_sample_rankings(
    77	                                    scores,
    78	                                    n_samples,
    79	                                    cutoff=cutoff+1,
    80	                                    return_gumbel=True)
    81	  else:
    82	    n_samples = sampled_rankings.shape[0]
    83	
    84	  delta_weights = rank_weights[:cutoff].copy()
    85	  if rank_weights.shape[0] < n_docs:
    86	    delta_weights[:-1] -= rank_weights[1:cutoff]
    87	  else:
    88	    delta_weights -= rank_weights[1:cutoff+1]
    89	
    90	  ranking_values = sampled_values[np.arange(n_samples)[:,None], sampled_rankings]
    91	  ranking_scores = scores[sampled_rankings[:,:cutoff]]
    92	
    93	  pairwise_diff = ranking_values[:,:cutoff,None] - scores[None,None,:]
    94	  pairwise_pdf = normal_pdf(pairwise_diff)
    95	
    96	  sampled_labels = labels[sampled_rankings]
    97	  delta_pairwise = delta_weights[None,:,None]*(labels[None,None,:] - sampled_labels[:,:cutoff,None])
    98	
    99	  sampled_grad = pairwise_pdf*delta_pairwise
   100	  sampled_mask = np.greater_equal(sampled_values[:,None,:], ranking_values[:,:cutoff,None])
   101	  sampled_grad[sampled_mask] = 0
   102	  sampled_grad = np.sum(sampled_grad, axis=1)
   103	
   104	  pairwise_diff = ranking_values[:,1:,None] - ranking_scores[:,None,:]
   105	  pairwise_pdf = normal_pdf(pairwise_diff)
   106	  delta_moved = delta_weights[None,:,None]*(sampled_labels[:,None,:cutoff] - sampled_labels[:,1:,None])
   107	
   108	  k_range = np.arange(cutoff)
   109	  k_mask = np.less_equal(k_range[:,None],k_range[None,:])[None,:,:]
   110	  grad_cor = pairwise_pdf*np.where(k_mask, delta_moved, 0)
   111	  grad_cor = np.sum(grad_cor, axis=1)

   113	  np.add.at(sampled_grad, (np.arange(n_samples)[:,None], sampled_rankings[:,:cutoff]), grad_cor)

     7	def sample_rankings(log_scores, n_samples, cutoff=None, prob_per_rank=False):
     8	  n_docs = log_scores.shape[0]
     9	  ind = np.arange(n_samples)
    10	
    11	  if cutoff:
    12	    ranking_len = min(n_docs, cutoff)
    13	  else:
    14	    ranking_len = n_docs
    15	
    16	  if prob_per_rank:
    17	    rank_prob_matrix = np.empty((ranking_len, n_docs), dtype=np.float64)
    18	
    19	  log_scores = np.tile(log_scores[None,:], (n_samples, 1))
    20	  rankings = np.empty((n_samples, ranking_len), dtype=np.int32)
    21	  inv_rankings = np.empty((n_samples, n_docs), dtype=np.int32)
    22	  rankings_prob = np.empty((n_samples, ranking_len), dtype=np.float64)
    23	
    24	  if cutoff:
    25	    inv_rankings[:] = ranking_len
    26	
    27	  for i in range(ranking_len):
    28	    log_scores += 18 - np.amax(log_scores, axis=1)[:, None]
    29	    log_denom = np.log(np.sum(np.exp(log_scores), axis=1))
    30	    probs = np.exp(log_scores - log_denom[:, None])
    31	    if prob_per_rank:
    32	      rank_prob_matrix[i, :] = np.mean(probs, axis=0)
    33	    cumprobs = np.cumsum(probs, axis=1)
    34	    random_values = np.random.uniform(size=n_samples)
    35	    greater_equal_mask = np.greater_equal(random_values[:,None], cumprobs)
    36	    sampled_ind = np.sum(greater_equal_mask, axis=1)
    37	
    38	    rankings[:, i] = sampled_ind
    39	    inv_rankings[ind, sampled_ind] = i
    40	    rankings_prob[:, i] = probs[ind, sampled_ind]
    41	    log_scores[ind, sampled_ind] = np.NINF
    42	
    43	  if prob_per_rank:
    44	    return rankings, inv_rankings, rankings_prob, rank_prob_matrix
    45	  else:
    46	    return rankings, inv_rankings, rankings_prob
    47	
    48	def gumbel_sample_rankings(log_scores, n_samples, cutoff=None, 
    49	                           inverted=False, doc_prob=False,
    50	                           prob_per_rank=False, return_gumbel=False,
    51	                           return_full_rankings=False):

    63	  gumbel_samples = np.random.gumbel(size=(n_samples, n_docs))
    64	  gumbel_scores = log_scores[None,:]+gumbel_samples

    88	
    89	  if return_gumbel:
    90	    gumbel_return_values = gumbel_scores
    91	  else:
    92	    gumbel_return_values = None

    95	    return rankings, inv_rankings, rankings_prob, rank_prob_matrix, gumbel_return_values
    96	  else:

    99	def normal_sample_rankings(log_scores, n_samples, cutoff=None, 
   100	                           inverted=False, return_gumbel=False,
   101	                           return_full_rankings=False):
   102	  n_docs = log_scores.shape[0]
   103	  ind = np.arange(n_samples)
   104	
   105	  if cutoff:
   106	    ranking_len = min(n_docs, cutoff)
   107	  else:
   108	    ranking_len = n_docs
   109	
   110	  normal_samples = np.random.normal(size=(n_samples, n_docs))
   111	  normal_scores = log_scores[None,:]+normal_samples
   112	
   113	  rankings, inv_rankings = rnk.multiple_cutoff_rankings(
   114	                                -normal_scores,

   116	                                invert=inverted,
   117	                                return_full_rankings=return_full_rankings)
   118	  if return_gumbel:
   119	    return rankings, inv_rankings, normal_scores

   123	def metrics_based_on_samples(sampled_rankings,
   124	                             weight_per_rank,
   125	                             addition_per_rank,
   126	                             weight_per_doc,):
   127	  cutoff = sampled_rankings.shape[1]
   128	  return np.sum(np.mean(
   129	              weight_per_doc[sampled_rankings]*weight_per_rank[None, :cutoff],
   130	            axis=0) + addition_per_rank[:cutoff], axis=0)
   131	
   132	def datasplit_metrics(data_split,
   133	                      policy_scores,
   134	                      weight_per_rank,
   135	                      addition_per_rank,
   136	                      weight_per_doc,
   137	                      query_norm_factors=None,
   138	                      n_samples=1000):
   139	  cutoff = weight_per_rank.shape[0]
   140	  n_queries = data_split.num_queries()
   141	  results = np.zeros((n_queries, weight_per_rank.shape[1]),)
   142	  for qid in range(n_queries):
   143	    q_doc_weights = data_split.query_values_from_vector(qid, weight_per_doc)
   144	    if not np.all(np.equal(q_doc_weights, 0.)):
   145	      q_policy_scores = data_split.query_values_from_vector(qid, policy_scores)
   146	      sampled_rankings = gumbel_sample_rankings(q_policy_scores,
   147	                                                n_samples,
   148	                                                cutoff=cutoff)[0]
   149	      results[qid] = metrics_based_on_samples(sampled_rankings,
   150	                                              weight_per_rank,
   151	                                              addition_per_rank,
   152	                                              q_doc_weights[:, None])
   153	  if query_norm_factors is not None:
   154	    results /= query_norm_factors
   155	
   156	  return np.mean(results, axis=0)

     4	import numpy as np
     5	
     6	def cutoff_ranking(scores, cutoff, invert=False):
     7	  n_docs = scores.shape[0]
     8	  cutoff = min(n_docs, cutoff)
     9	  full_partition = np.argpartition(scores, cutoff-1)
    10	  partition = full_partition[:cutoff]
    11	  sorted_partition = np.argsort(scores[partition])
    12	  ranked_partition = partition[sorted_partition]
    13	  if not invert:
    14	    return ranked_partition
    15	  else:
    16	    full_partition[:cutoff] = ranked_partition
    17	    inverted = np.empty(n_docs, dtype=ranked_partition.dtype)
    18	    inverted[full_partition] = np.arange(n_docs)
    19	    return ranked_partition, inverted
    20	
    21	def multiple_cutoff_rankings(scores, cutoff, invert=True, return_full_rankings=False):
    22	  n_samples = scores.shape[0]
    23	  n_docs = scores.shape[1]
    24	  cutoff = min(n_docs, cutoff)
    25	
    26	  ind = np.arange(n_samples)
    27	  partition = np.argpartition(scores, cutoff-1, axis=1)
    28	  sorted_partition = np.argsort(scores[ind[:, None], partition[:,:cutoff]], axis=1)
    29	  rankings = partition[ind[:, None], sorted_partition]
    30	
    31	  if not invert:
    32	    inverted = None
    33	  else:
    34	    inverted = np.full((n_samples, n_docs), cutoff, dtype=rankings.dtype)
    35	    inverted[ind[:, None], rankings] = np.arange(cutoff)[None, :]
    36	  
    37	  if return_full_rankings:
    38	    partition[:,:cutoff] = rankings
    39	    rankings = partition
    40	
    41	  return rankings, inverted
    42	
    43	def rank_and_invert(scores, tiebreakers=None):
    44	  n_docs = scores.shape[0]
    45	  noise = np.random.uniform(size=n_docs)
    46	  if tiebreakers is not None:
    47	    rank_ind = np.lexsort((noise, tiebreakers, scores))[::-1]
    48	  else:
    49	    rank_ind = np.lexsort((noise, scores))[::-1]
    50	  inverted = np.empty(n_docs, dtype=rank_ind.dtype)
    51	  inverted[rank_ind] = np.arange(n_docs)
    52	  return rank_ind, inverted
    53	
    54	def data_split_rank_and_invert(scores, data_split, tiebreakers=None):
    55	  ranking = np.zeros(data_split.num_docs(), dtype=np.int64)
    56	  inverted = np.zeros(data_split.num_docs(), dtype=np.int64)
    57	  for qid in np.arange(data_split.num_queries()):
    58	    s_i, e_i = data_split.doclist_ranges[qid:qid+2]
    59	    q_scores = scores[s_i:e_i]
    60	    if tiebreakers is not None:
    61	      q_tiebreakers = tiebreakers[s_i:e_i]
    62	      (ranking[s_i:e_i],
    63	       inverted[s_i:e_i]) = rank_and_invert(
    64	                              q_scores,
    65	                              tiebreakers=q_tiebreakers
    66	                            )
    67	    else:
    68	      (ranking[s_i:e_i],
    69	       inverted[s_i:e_i]) = rank_and_invert(q_scores)
    70	  return ranking, inverted
    71	
    72	def data_split_model_rank_and_invert(model, data_split):
    73	  scores = model_score(model, data_split.feature_matrix)
    74	  return data_split_rank_and_invert(scores, data_split)
    75	
    76	def data_split_rank_and_invert_tiebreak_model(scores, model, data_split):
    77	  model_scores = model_score(model, data_split.feature_matrix)
    78	  return data_split_rank_and_invert(scores, data_split, tiebreakers=model_scores)
    79	
    80	def data_split_nn_rank_and_invert(model, data_split):
    81	  scores = model(data_split.feature_matrix)[:, 0]
    82	  return data_split_rank_and_invert(scores, data_split)
    83	
    84	def data_split_rank_and_invert_tiebreak_nn(scores, model, data_split):
    85	  model_scores = model(data_split.feature_matrix)[:, 0]
    86	  return data_split_rank_and_invert(scores, data_split, tiebreakers=model_scores)
    87	
    88	def model_score(model, doc_feat):
    89	  return np.dot(doc_feat, model)
    90	
    91	def model_rank_and_invert(model, doc_feat):
    92	  scores = model_score(model, doc_feat)
    93	  return rank_and_invert(scores)

41:This paper addresses both of these shortcomings: our main contribution is the novel PL-Rank-3 algorithm that computes the same approximation as PL-Rank-2 while minimizing its computational complexity. PL-Rank-3 has computional costs in the same order as the best sorting algorithms; we posit that this is the lowest order possible for a metric-based LTR method. Our experimental comparison includes both the previous PL-Rank-2 and StochasticRank as baselines, providing the first comparison between these two methods. The introduction of PL-Rank-3 is exciting for the LTR field, as it pushes the limit of the minimal computational complexity that LTR methods can have. Potentially, it may enable future LTR to be applied to much larger scales than currently feasible.
